cue: Read the element sitting at the final index.

prompt: Write nthFromEnd(a, k) that returns the k-th element counting from the end of the array (k = 1 is the last element). Do not use .at().
cue: Turn the distance-from-end into an index by subtracting from the length.
Count backward from the end by the given amount.
Ask whether an array has a real slot at a negative bracket index.
syntax: arr[-1] is a property access, not a position

prompt: Write ringGet(a, i) that returns the element at position i, wrapping back to the start when i runs past the end. Assume i is a non-negative integer and a is non-empty.
cue: Wrap the index back into range using the remainder against the length.

prompt: Write subrange(a, i, j) that returns a new array holding the elements from index i up to but not including j. Do not use .slice.
cue: Loop from i while below j, collecting each element into a fresh array.
Take the half-open range from the start index to the end index.
Consider what slice yields when the start sits past the end.
A negative end argument is measured back from the array's end.
Ask whether writing into the sliced copy reaches back to the source.

prompt: Write reverseRange(a, i, j) that reverses only the elements from index i to index j inclusive, in place, and returns a. Use two pointers; do not use .reverse().
cue: Point one index at i and one at j, then swap and step both inward until they cross.
syntax: swap a[lo] and a[hi], then lo++, hi--
mapsTo: LC 344 · Reverse String (the two-pointer in-place reversal, over a sub-range here)

prompt: Write isPalindrome(a) that returns true when the array reads the same forward and backward. Use two pointers; do not reverse the array.
cue: Compare the outermost pair, then move both pointers inward.
Ask whether reverse edits the array in place or leaves it alone.
The spread builds a separate array before the reverse runs.

prompt: Write everyKth(a, k) that returns a new array of every k-th element starting at index 0. Use an index loop with stride k; do not use .filter.
cue: Walk an index that advances by k each iteration.
syntax: for (let t = 0; t < n; t += k)

cue: Keep only the positions whose index is even.

prompt: Write window(a, i, k) that returns a new array of the k elements starting at index i. Assume i + k <= a.length. Do not use .slice.
cue: Read k elements in a row, offsetting each from the start index.
Slice from the start index to the start plus the width.

prompt: Write maxWindowSum(a, k) that returns the largest sum of any k consecutive elements. Do not re-sum each window from scratch. Assume 1 <= k <= a.length.
cue: Add up the first k elements, then slide the window forward one step at a time and keep the largest sum you see.
syntax: add the entering element, subtract the leaving one
note: Big-O describes how the work grows as the array size n grows. Rolling the sum (add the entering element, drop the leaving one) does one step per element, so the whole scan is O(n): the work grows in step with n. Re-summing each window from scratch does k additions per window, which is O(nk).

prompt: Write chunk(a, k) that splits a into consecutive sub-arrays of length k, the last one shorter if the array does not divide evenly.
cue: Step the start by k, grabbing one piece per step.
Ask which elements splice removes and whether it edits the array itself.

prompt: Write midpoint(lo, hi) that returns the integer index halfway between lo and hi (rounded down).
cue: Average the two bounds, then round down to an integer.
The shift halves the span; add it onto the lower bound.
note: Adding half the span instead of averaging the ends keeps lo+hi from overflowing a fixed-width int in C or Java; in JS it is a good habit and stays exact below 2^53.

cue: Find the middle index, check its value against the target, then move the lower bound just past it.

concept: binary search on a sorted array
prompt: Write binarySearch(a, target) that returns the index of target in the ascending-sorted array a, or -1 if it is absent. Halve the search range each step; do not use .indexOf.
cue: Track a lo/hi range; compare the middle element and drop the half that cannot hold the target.
note: Each comparison discards half the remaining range, so lookup is O(log n) instead of an O(n) linear scan.
